The direct-address table
(cid:73) U = {0, 1, ..., m − 1} the universe set of keys;
(cid:73) An array T [0..m − 1]:
T [k] =
(cid:26) x
NULL
if x ∈ S and x.key = k
otherwise.
(cid:73) Each position (slot) in the array corresponds to a key in the universe
U.
(cid:73) If |S| = n, then n ≤ m.
FII, UAIC
Lecture 11
DS 2018/2019
4 / 29

The direct-address table - Operations
(cid:73) Operations
Function search(T , k)
begin
return T [k]
end
Procedure insert(T , x)
begin
T [x.key ] = x
end
Procedure delete(T , x)
begin
T [x.key ] = NULL
end
(cid:73) The time complexity of operations: Θ(1)
FII, UAIC
Lecture 11
DS 2018/2019
5 / 29

The direct-address table
(cid:73) The memory space: Θ(|U|).
(cid:73) Problems:
(cid:73) the keys can be non integers;
(cid:73) the domain of keys is very large:
(cid:73) numbers on 64 bits (18.446.744.073.709.551.616 of diﬀerent keys)
(cid:73) strings;
(cid:73) the set of stored keys is very small relative to U.
(cid:73) Solution: hash tables
(cid:73) a generalization of the concept of direct-address table;
(cid:73) an eﬃcient data structure for implementing dictionaries.
FII, UAIC
Lecture 11
DS 2018/2019

Hash tables
(cid:73) It uses a hash function h to associate to the keys of universe U a
value from the set {0, 1, · · · , m − 1}.
(cid:73) An element with the key k has associated the position h(k) in the
table T .
(cid:73) The hash function reduces the domains of indices and implicitly the
size of the stored array.
(cid:73) Collision: ∃ x1, x2 ∈ S such that h(x1.key ) = h(x2.key )
FII, UAIC
Lecture 11
DS 2018/2019
8 / 29

Collision resolution by chaining
(cid:73) The records that have associated the same slot will be stored in a
linked list. T becomes an array of pointers.
(cid:73) A simple solution, but it requires additional memory space.
(cid:73) Worst case scenario: all keys have associated the same slot
(cid:73) the access time: Θ(n).
FII, UAIC
Lecture 11
DS 2018/2019
10 / 29

Chaining – Operations
Function search(T , k)
begin
search for the element with the key k in the list T [h(k)]
end
Procedure insert(T , x)
begin
insert x at the beginning of the list T [h(x.cheie)]
end
Procedure delete(T , x)
begin
delete x from the list T [h(x.cheie)]
end
FII, UAIC
Lecture 11
DS 2018/2019
11 / 29

Chaining – Complexity analysis
(cid:73) Search:
The worst case complexity depends on the length of the list.
(cid:73) Insertion:
The worst case complexity: O(1).
(cid:73) Deletion:
O(1) for doubly linked lists; for simple linked lists, ﬁrst search x and
store his predecessor in order to restore the link.
FII, UAIC
Lecture 11
DS 2018/2019
12 / 29

Chaining – The average case complexity analysis
(cid:73) The assumption of simple uniform hashing: each key k ∈ U has an
equal probability to be stored in any location in the table T and
independently of the locations of other keys.
(cid:73) The load factor of the table T is
α = n/m,
where n is the number of keys (|S|), and m is the number of locations
(the size of the array T ).
(cid:73) The time to compute the hash function is Θ(1).
FII, UAIC
Lecture 11
DS 2018/2019
13 / 29

Chaining – The average case complexity analysis
Theorem:
In a hash table in which collisions are resolved by chaining, an
unsuccessful search takes average case time Θ(1 + α), under the
assumption of simple uniform hashing.
Theorem:
In a hash table in which collisions are resolved by chaining, a successful
search takes average case time Θ(1 + α), under the assumption of simple
uniform hashing.
Corollary:
If the number of slots is at least proportional to the number of elements
(n = O(m) or, equivalently, α = O(1)), then the search operation has the
complexity, in average, O(1).
FII, UAIC
Lecture 11
DS 2018/2019
14 / 29

The hash function
(cid:73) Deterministic: for a key k, the function must provide always the same
value h(k).
(cid:73) Random: aims to minimize collisions.
(cid:73) A good hash function distributes the keys uniformly in the locations
of the table.
(cid:73) The assumption of simple uniform hashing is diﬃcult to guarantee,
but there are heuristic techniques that work well in practice (as long
as their shortcomings can be avoided).
FII, UAIC
Lecture 11
DS 2018/2019
16 / 29

Hash functions – The division method
h(k) = k mod m
(cid:73) Assume that all keys are natural numbers.
(cid:73) if the keys are not natural numbers, then we must ﬁnd a way to
interpret them as natural numbers;
(cid:73) Example: suppose an identiﬁer of the form (112, 116); in the base 128,
it becomes (112 × 128) + 116 = 14452.
(cid:73) Do not choose for m a value with a small divisor d. The predominance
of congruent modulo d keys can aﬀect negatively the uniformity.
(cid:73) If m = 2r , then the value of the function depends only on the lasts r
bits of k.
(cid:73) Example: k = 1011000111011010 and r = 6 (cid:55)→ h(k) = 011010.
(cid:73) Choose m a prime number that is not close to a power of 2 or 10.
FII, UAIC
Lecture 11
DS 2018/2019
17 / 29

Hash functions – The multiplication method
h(k) = (cid:98)m(kA − (cid:98)kA(cid:99))(cid:99)
(cid:73) A ∈ (0, 1) is a constant.
(cid:73) The value of m is not critical (usually a power of 2).
h(k) = (kA mod 2w )rsh(w − r )
(cid:73) m = 2r , (machine with words of w –bits).
(cid:73) A is an odd integer in the range (2w −1, 2w ).
(cid:73) rsh is the bitwise right shift operator.
FII, UAIC
Lecture 11
DS 2018/2019
18 / 29

Hash functions – The multiplication method
(cid:73) Example: m = 23 and words on w = 7 bits.
(cid:73) Do not choose A too close to 2w −1 or 2w .
(cid:73) Knuth: A = (
√
5 − 1)/2.
(cid:73) The multiplication modulo 2w is faster compared to the division; the
operator rsh is fast.
FII, UAIC
Lecture 11
DS 2018/2019
19 / 29

Hash functions – Universal hashing
h(k) = [(ak + b) mod p] mod m
(cid:73) p a prime number with p > |U|;
(cid:73) a, b random numbers in {0, ..., p − 1}.
(cid:73) k1 (cid:54)= k2, Pra,b{h(k1) = h(k2)} = 1/m.
FII, UAIC
Lecture 11

Solving collisions by open addressing
(cid:73) All items are stored inside the table T ; no additional memory space is
used, except for the hash table.
(cid:73) The insert function examines the table until an empty location is
found.
(cid:73) The hash function depends on the key as well as on the number of
examination:
h : U × {0, 1, ..., m − 1} (cid:55)→ {0, 1, ..., m − 1}
(cid:73) The sequence of examinations (probe sequence)
< h(k, 0), h(k, 1), · · · , h(k, m − 1) > must be a permutation of
{0, 1, .., m − 1}.
(cid:73) Disadvantages: the table can be ﬁlled; the deletion may become
diﬃcult.
FII, UAIC
Lecture 11
DS 2018/2019
22 / 29

Open addressing – Operations
Function search(T , k)
begin
i ← 0
repeat
j ← h(k, i)
if T [j] == k then
return j
else
i ← i + 1
until T [j] == NULL OR i == m;
return NULL
end
FII, UAIC
Lecture 11
DS 2018/2019
23 / 29

Open addressing – Operations
Function insert(T , k)
begin
i ← 0
repeat
j ← h(k, i)
if T [j] == NULL then
T [j] ← k
return j
else
i ← i + 1
until i == m;
return −1
end
FII, UAIC
Lecture 11
DS 2018/2019
24 / 29

Open addressing – Strategies for probing
Linear probing:
h(k, i) = (h(cid:48)(k) + i) mod m
(cid:73) h(cid:48)(k) an ordinary hash function.
(cid:73) For a key k, the probe sequence is
h(cid:48)(k), h(cid:48)(k) + 1, h(cid:48)(k) + 2, ..., m − 1, 0, 1, ..., h(cid:48)(k) − 1.
(cid:73) Advantage: a simple method.
(cid:73) Disadvantage: primary clustering – long strings of occupied slots
build up, increasing the average search time.
FII, UAIC
Lecture 11
DS 2018/2019
25 / 29

Open addressing – Strategies for probing
Quadratic probing:
h(k, i) = (h(cid:48)(k) + c1i + c2i 2) mod m
(cid:73) h(cid:48)(k) an ordinary hash function.
(cid:73) For a key k, the ﬁrst location probed is h(cid:48)(k), and the next positions
probed are oﬀset by amounts that depend in a quadratic manner on
the previously probed position.
(cid:73) Disadvantage: secondary clustering – if two keys have the same initial
probe position, then their probe sequences are the same.
(cid:73) It works better than linear probing.
FII, UAIC
Lecture 11
DS 2018/2019
26 / 29

Open addressing – Strategies for probing
Double hashing:
h(k, i) = (h1(k) + ih2(k)) mod m
(cid:73) h1(k) si h2(k) two ordinary hash functions.
(cid:73) For a key k, the ﬁrst location probed is h1(k), and the next positions
probed are oﬀset by h2(k) mod m towards the previous position.
(cid:73) This method has in general good results, assuming that h2(k) is
relatively prime to m. One way to accomplish this is to consider m a
power of 2 and to choose h2(k) such that to result only odd numbers.
FII, UAIC
Lecture 11
DS 2018/2019
27 / 29

Open addressing – Complexity analysis
The uniform hashing assumption: each key is equally likely to have any of
the m! permutations as probe sequence.
Theorem:
Given an open-address hash table with load factor α < 1, assuming
uniform hashing, the average number of probes is at most
(cid:73)
(cid:73) 1
1
1−α in an unsuccessful search, and
α ln 1
1−α in a successful search.
Corollary:
If α is constant, then accessing an open-address hash table requires in
average a constant time, Θ(1).
FII, UAIC
Lecture 11
DS 2018/2019
28 / 29

Applications
(cid:73) Hash tables are used for: database indexing, compilers - symbol
tables, caches, etc.
(cid:73) Applications of hash functions: CRC, Cryptographic hash functions,
etc.
FII, UAIC
Lecture 11
